pitch.summary: Express is the "fast, unopinionated, minimalist web framework for Node.js" — this is the 5.x line (package version 5.2.1). The entire framework is roughly 2,300 lines of JavaScript across six files in lib/: a factory (express.js) that builds an app function, a settings-and-dispatch layer (application.js), request and response prototypes that decorate Node's native http objects (request.js, response.js), a template-lookup abstraction (view.js), and helpers (utils.js). Its defining move is composition over inheritance: rather than subclass anything, Express creates prototype objects with Object.create and swaps them onto each incoming req/res via Object.setPrototypeOf, then delegates the hard parts — routing, body parsing, static files, file streaming — to small single-purpose npm packages it depends on.
pitch.audience: JavaScript/Node.js developers who use Express and want to understand how the framework actually works under the hood, plus contributors to the framework itself. Reading it rewards anyone curious how a minimalist library decorates Node's raw http server.

techStack.name: JavaScript
techStack.role: The whole framework is ES5-flavored CommonJS JavaScript ('use strict', var, prototype objects) — deliberately plain so it runs on Node 18+ with no build step.

techStack.name: Node.js
techStack.role: Target runtime (engines: node >= 18). Express decorates node:http's IncomingMessage/ServerResponse prototypes and creates the server via http.createServer(app).

techStack.name: router
techStack.role: In v5 the routing engine was extracted into this standalone package; express.js and application.js require('router') for the Router and Route constructors instead of shipping their own.

techStack.name: body-parser
techStack.role: Provides express.json / express.raw / express.text / express.urlencoded — express.js re-exports its parsers directly as the framework's built-in body middleware.

techStack.name: serve-static
techStack.role: Backs express.static for serving files from a directory; re-exported unchanged from lib/express.js.

techStack.name: send
techStack.role: Streams files with HTTP caching/range support; res.sendFile and res.download in response.js delegate to it.

techStack.name: finalhandler
techStack.role: The default terminal handler app.handle installs when no callback is given — writes 404s and surfaces errors that fall through the middleware stack.

techStack.name: Mocha
techStack.role: Test runner for the whole suite; `npm test` runs mocha over test/ and test/acceptance/ with --check-leaks.

techStack.name: SuperTest
techStack.role: Drives the app as a real HTTP server in tests, asserting on responses (used throughout test/res.*.js and acceptance tests).

techStack.name: ESLint
techStack.role: Lints the codebase; `npm run lint` runs `eslint .` and is a separate required CI job from the test matrix.

architecture.title: A factory that decorates plain functions, not classes
architecture.body: Express has no classes. `express()` (lib/express.js:36) returns a function `app(req, res, next)` whose only job is to call `app.handle`. That bare function is then turned into the application by `merge-descriptors` copying two prototypes onto it: `EventEmitter.prototype` (so an app can `emit`/`on`, e.g. the `mount` event) and the `application` object from lib/application.js (all the `app.set`, `app.use`, `app.get`, `app.listen` methods). The request and response prototypes are attached to the app too, as `app.request`/`app.response`, each created with `Object.create` over the base `req`/`res` objects so per-app customization never mutates the shared prototype. Understanding this one file explains the whole object model: everything else is a prototype that gets mixed or swapped in.

diagram.title: Module wiring at require time
diagram.source:
flowchart TD index["index.js"] --> express["lib/express.js
createApplication()"] express -->|mixin proto| application["lib/application.js
app prototype"] express -->|app.request| request["lib/request.js
req prototype"] express -->|app.response| response["lib/response.js
res prototype"] express -->|Router, Route| router["router (npm)"] express -->|json/raw/text/urlencoded| bodyparser["body-parser (npm)"] express -->|static| servestatic["serve-static (npm)"] application --> view["lib/view.js"] application --> utils["lib/utils.js"] application -->|new Router| router application -->|default handler| finalhandler["finalhandler (npm)"] response -->|sendFile/download| send["send (npm)"] request -.extends.-> http["node:http prototypes"] response -.extends.-> http

architecture.title: Per-request prototype swapping is the whole request lifecycle
architecture.body: When a request arrives, `app.handle` (lib/application.js:152) does something unusual: it takes Node's raw `req`/`res` and calls `Object.setPrototypeOf(req, this.request)` and `Object.setPrototypeOf(res, this.response)`. That single line is why `req.query`, `res.send`, and friends exist — they were never subclassed onto the objects; the app's prototype is grafted on at dispatch time. `handle` also sets the `X-Powered-By` header, wires the circular `req.res`/`res.req` references, ensures `res.locals`, and installs `finalhandler` as the terminal callback, then hands off to `this.router.handle(req, res, done)`. The base router is created lazily the first time `this.router` is read (application.js:69), configured from the `case sensitive routing` and `strict routing` settings.

diagram.title: One request through app.handle
diagram.source:
sequenceDiagram
  actor Client
  participant Node as node:http server
  participant App as app (fn)
  participant H as app.handle
  participant R as router (npm)
  participant MW as middleware / route
  Client->>Node: HTTP request
  Node->>App: app(req, res)
  App->>H: app.handle(req, res)
  H->>H: setHeader X-Powered-By
  H->>H: setPrototypeOf(req, app.request)
  H->>H: setPrototypeOf(res, app.response)
  H->>R: this.router.handle(req, res, done)
  R->>MW: run matching stack
  MW->>MW: res.send / res.json / res.render
  MW-->>Client: response
  Note over H,R: if nothing responds, finalhandler(done) writes 404/error

architecture.title: req and res decorate node:http, not replace it
architecture.body: lib/request.js starts with `var req = Object.create(http.IncomingMessage.prototype)` (line 30) and lib/response.js with `var res = Object.create(http.ServerResponse.prototype)` (line 43). So Express's request/response objects inherit every native Node method and add sugar on top. Request adds mostly lazy getters via a local `defineGetter` helper — `req.query` (parses the URL with the configured query-parser fn), `req.protocol`/`req.ip`/`req.hostname`/`req.subdomains` (all consult the compiled `trust proxy fn`), and content-negotiation helpers (`req.accepts`, `req.is`) that delegate to the `accepts` and `type-is` packages. Response adds the high-level senders — `res.send` (response.js:126) is the spine: it infers Content-Type, computes Content-Length, generates an ETag, honors conditional-GET freshness (304), and special-cases HEAD/204/205 before calling `res.end`. `res.json`, `res.jsonp`, `res.sendStatus`, and `res.redirect` all funnel back through `res.send`.

diagram.title: res.send: the funnel every body-sender goes through
diagram.source:
flowchart TD
  json["res.json"] --> send["res.send (response.js:126)"]
  jsonp["res.jsonp"] --> send
  sendStatus["res.sendStatus"] --> send
  redirect["res.redirect"] --> send
  send --> ctype{"Content-Type set?"}
  ctype -->|no| infer["infer type (html/json/bin)"]
  ctype -->|yes| len["compute Content-Length"]
  infer --> len
  len --> etag["generate ETag (etag fn)"]
  etag --> fresh{"req.fresh?"}
  fresh -->|yes| s304["status 304, strip body"]
  fresh -->|no| end["res.end(chunk)"]
  s304 --> end

architecture.title: Settings, compiled options, and delegated concerns
architecture.body: Configuration is a plain key/value bag: `app.set(key, val)` writes `this.settings[key]` and, for three special keys, compiles a companion function via lib/utils.js — `etag` -> `etag fn` (compileETag), `query parser` -> `query parser fn` (compileQueryParser), and `trust proxy` -> `trust proxy fn` (compileTrust, which understands booleans, hop counts, IP/CIDR strings, and delegates to proxy-addr). `app.defaultConfiguration` (application.js:90) seeds sane defaults (`x-powered-by` on, weak etags, `views` = ./views, view cache on in production). The verb methods (`app.get`, `app.post`, ...) are generated in a loop over `utils.methods` and simply proxy to `this.router.route(path)[method]`, with `app.get` special-cased so a one-argument call reads a setting instead. Rendering is delegated too: `res.render` -> `app.render` -> a `View` (lib/view.js) that resolves the template file and lazily `require()`s the engine by extension via its `__express` export.

diagram.title: app.set compiles companion functions
diagram.source:
flowchart LR
  set["app.set(key, val)"] --> store["settings[key] = val"]
  store --> sw{"which key?"}
  sw -->|etag| ce["compileETag -> 'etag fn'"]
  sw -->|query parser| cq["compileQueryParser -> 'query parser fn'"]
  sw -->|trust proxy| ct["compileTrust -> 'trust proxy fn'"]
  ct --> pa["proxy-addr (npm)"]
  cq --> qs["qs (npm, extended mode)"]

nodes.label: index.js
nodes.description: One-line entrypoint: re-exports lib/express.js as the package's public API.

nodes.label: lib/express.js
nodes.description: createApplication() factory; mixes the app prototype onto a function and re-exports Router, Route, and the body/static middleware.

nodes.label: lib/application.js
nodes.description: The app prototype: settings (set/get/enable), use/route/param, per-request dispatch in app.handle, listen, render.

nodes.label: lib/request.js
nodes.description: req prototype over http.IncomingMessage; lazy getters for query, protocol, ip, host, and accepts/is negotiation helpers.

nodes.label: lib/response.js
nodes.description: res prototype over http.ServerResponse; the send/json/jsonp/sendFile/render/redirect/cookie helpers.

nodes.label: lib/view.js
nodes.description: View class: resolves a template file on disk and lazily loads its engine via the engine's __express export.

nodes.label: lib/utils.js
nodes.description: Private helpers: HTTP method list, ETag generators, and compileETag/compileQueryParser/compileTrust option compilers.

nodes.label: node:http
nodes.description: Node core HTTP module; provides IncomingMessage/ServerResponse prototypes Express extends and createServer for app.listen.

nodes.label: router
nodes.description: Standalone routing engine (extracted from Express in v5); supplies Router and Route.

nodes.label: body-parser
nodes.description: Request body parsers re-exported as express.json/raw/text/urlencoded.

nodes.label: serve-static
nodes.description: Static-file middleware re-exported as express.static.

nodes.label: finalhandler
nodes.description: Default terminal handler that writes 404s and surfaces unhandled errors.

nodes.label: send
nodes.description: File streaming with caching/range support behind res.sendFile and res.download.

nodes.label: accepts
nodes.description: Content negotiation used by req.accepts / acceptsEncodings / acceptsCharsets / acceptsLanguages.

nodes.label: type-is
nodes.description: Content-Type matching behind req.is.

nodes.label: proxy-addr
nodes.description: Trusted-proxy address resolution used by req.ip/ips and compileTrust.

nodes.label: qs
nodes.description: Extended query-string parser used when 'query parser' is set to 'extended'.

nodes.label: merge-descriptors
nodes.description: Copies prototype descriptors; mixes EventEmitter and the app prototype onto the app function.

nodes.label: etag
nodes.description: Generates strong/weak ETags for res.send via the utils ETag generators.

nodes.label: debug
nodes.description: Namespaced debug logging (express:application, express:view).

codebaseMap.purpose: The entire framework. Six files, ~2,300 LOC: the factory, the app prototype, the req/res prototypes, the view abstraction, and helpers. Everything a newcomer needs to understand Express lives here.

keyFiles.note: createApplication() factory and the module's public exports (Router, static, json...).
app prototype: settings, use/route/verb methods, app.handle dispatch, listen.
req prototype over http.IncomingMessage; lazy getters and negotiation helpers.
View: template file resolution and lazy engine loading.
method list, ETag generators, and the compileETag/QueryParser/Trust option compilers.

codebaseMap.purpose: The package entrypoint declared in package.json. A single require that re-exports lib/express.js, so `require('express')` returns createApplication.
The Mocha suite, one file per public method (req.*.js, res.*.js, app.*.js) plus Router/Route unit tests. These are the clearest executable spec of intended behavior and drive the app with SuperTest.

keyFiles.note: Largest test file (940 LOC): routing behavior and edge cases.
Exercises the res.send funnel: types, ETags, freshness, HEAD.
Required before the suite (sets NODE_ENV=test) via the mocha --require flag.

codebaseMap.purpose: Acceptance tests that run each example app end-to-end, guaranteeing the examples in examples/ actually work and doubling as integration coverage.

keyFiles.note: Drives the web-service example against a real server.

codebaseMap.purpose: ~25 runnable example apps (auth, mvc, route-separation, sessions, content-negotiation...) each with its own views/. The best place to learn idiomatic Express usage; every example is covered by an acceptance test.

keyFiles.note: The minimal app: express(), app.get('/'), app.listen(3000).
A larger example showing routing, controllers, and view rendering.

codebaseMap.purpose: CI definitions. ci.yml runs a separate lint job plus a test matrix across Node 18-26 on Ubuntu and Windows; legacy.yml, codeql.yml, and scorecard.yml add older-Node, security-scan, and supply-chain checks.

keyFiles.note: Lint job (npm run lint) + test matrix (npm test) — the checks a PR must pass.

tour.title: The entrypoint and the factory
tour.why: Start where `require('express')` lands. index.js just forwards to lib/express.js, whose createApplication() is the seed of the entire object model — read it first because every other file is a prototype this factory wires together.
tour.notice: That `app` is a plain function delegating to app.handle, that merge-descriptors mixes EventEmitter + the application prototype onto it, and that Router/static/json are simply re-exported from npm packages — Express is largely a curator of small modules.

tour.title: How settings and the default config work
tour.why: Before dispatch, understand the settings bag every request will consult. defaultConfiguration seeds the defaults and app.set shows the compile-on-write trick, so later references to 'trust proxy fn' or 'etag fn' won't be mysterious.
tour.notice: That app.set stores a value AND, for etag/query parser/trust proxy, compiles a companion function; and that defaults like view cache flip on only when NODE_ENV is production.

tour.title: The request lifecycle: app.handle
tour.why: This is the spine of the framework and the payoff of steps 1-2: it is where a raw Node req/res becomes an Express req/res and control passes to the router. Read app.init alongside it to see the lazy base-router getter it hands off to.
tour.notice: The two Object.setPrototypeOf calls that graft the app's request/response prototypes onto the native objects, and that finalhandler is the default terminal callback when nothing in the stack responds.

tour.title: Mounting middleware and generating the verb methods
tour.why: Now that dispatch is clear, see how routes and middleware get registered in the first place. app.use handles the path/app-mount cases; the methods.forEach loop shows app.get/post/... are thin proxies to the router — demystifying the API surface most users touch.
tour.notice: That app.use special-cases mounting a sub-app (emitting 'mount' and restoring req.app), and that app.get is overloaded: one argument reads a setting, more than one defines a route.

tour.title: req: decorating http.IncomingMessage
tour.why: With dispatch understood, look at what req actually gains. Reading the prototype creation plus a couple of getters (query, protocol) shows the lazy-getter pattern and how getters lean on app settings — the same shape repeats for ip, host, subdomains.
tour.notice: That req inherits from IncomingMessage.prototype (no subclass), that getters are defined with a local defineGetter helper, and that protocol/ip/host all consult the compiled 'trust proxy fn' from step 2.

tour.title: res.send: the response funnel
tour.why: res.send is the single most important method to understand on the response side: json, jsonp, sendStatus, and redirect all funnel through it. Read it after req so you see both halves of a request/response, and see how much HTTP correctness lives in one function.
tour.notice: The Content-Type inference, the Content-Length + ETag generation, the req.fresh -> 304 short-circuit, and the HEAD/204/205 body-stripping — and that res.json just stringifies then calls res.send.

tour.title: Views and file transfer at the edges
tour.why: Finish with the two delegated concerns you have not yet seen: template rendering and file streaming. These sit at the boundary and hand off to app.render/View and the send package respectively, rounding out the mental model of what Express does itself vs. delegates.
tour.notice: That res.render is a thin wrapper over app.render + View, that View lazily require()s an engine by file extension via its __express export, and that res.sendFile just configures and pipes the `send` stream.

tour.title: The tests as executable spec
tour.why: Close the loop by reading a test file for a method you just studied. The suite is organized one-file-per-method and driven with SuperTest, so it is the most precise statement of intended behavior and the place your own changes will need coverage.
tour.notice: How SuperTest boots the app as a real server and asserts on status/headers/body, and that test/support/env.js (loaded via mocha --require) forces NODE_ENV=test so error logging stays quiet.

entries.insight: By far the most-touched file, which for a library is meaningful signal: this is where the 27 runtime dependencies get version-bumped. Express's surface area is small precisely because so much behavior lives in those deps, so most 'Express' changes are actually dependency updates recorded here.
The hand-maintained changelog. Its churn tracks release cadence; skim the top entries to see what the maintainers consider a user-facing change and how v5 differs from v4.

The most-churned source file and the largest (908 LOC). It concentrates HTTP-correctness edge cases (ETags, ranges, cookies, JSONP security mitigations), so it is both central and the file most likely to have a subtle bug or a follow-up fix — read it carefully before changing send/json.

Still actively edited because it holds the dispatch path and the settings system. Changes here ripple through every request, which is why they land with test updates — a good file to watch for how core behavior evolves.
Recent activity clusters around the trust-proxy-dependent getters (protocol, host, ip). If you see security or reverse-proxy fixes, this file and compileTrust in utils.js are the usual pair.

Moderately churned historically but quiet lately, matching its role: the compile* option helpers are stable infrastructure. Safe ground to read to learn how settings become functions without expecting it to shift under you.

The largest and most-edited test file, but now dormant — a sign routing behavior settled once routing itself was extracted into the external `router` package in v5. Its history is a good record of past routing edge cases.

hotspots.interpretation: The churn splits cleanly into three stories. The genuine top-of-list files (package.json, History.md, CI workflows) reflect that a mature framework's day-to-day work is dependency bumps and release housekeeping, not core rewrites. Among source files, lib/response.js dominates — it is the largest file and the richest in HTTP edge cases, so a newcomer should treat it as the highest-risk area. And the once-hot test/app.router.js going dormant is itself informative: routing was moved out to the `router` dependency in v5, so the framework's own code is now smaller and steadier than its history suggests.

prerequisites: Node.js >= 18 (package.json engines; CI tests Node 18 through 26)
npm (ships with Node); no other toolchain or database is required

setup.title: Clone the repository
setup.notes: Express itself has no build step — it is plain CommonJS JavaScript run directly by Node.

setup.title: Install dependencies
setup.notes: Installs the 27 runtime deps plus dev tooling (mocha, supertest, eslint, ejs/hbs for example views). CI uses `npm install --ignore-scripts --include=dev`; plain `npm install` is fine locally.

run.title: Run an example app
run.notes: There is no `npm start` script — Express is a library, not an app. The examples/ apps are the way to run real servers; hello-world listens on http://localhost:3000.

run.title: Use it as a dependency
run.notes: In a consumer project: `const app = require('express')()` then `app.get('/', (req,res) => res.send('Hello World'))` and `app.listen(3000)` (see Readme.md).

test.title: Run the full test suite
test.notes: Runs `mocha --require test/support/env --reporter spec --check-leaks test/ test/acceptance/`. No database or services needed.

test.title: Run tests with coverage
test.notes: Wraps the suite in nyc and writes an HTML coverage report; `npm run test-ci` produces lcov for CI.

test.title: Lint
test.notes: Runs `eslint .`. This is a separate required CI job from the test matrix; `npm run lint:fix` autofixes.

firstTasks.title: Add or extend a res.* unit test
firstTasks.description: Pick a response helper you understand from the tour (e.g. res.send or res.json) and add a test case in the matching test/res.*.js file for a behavior branch that interests you, running it with `npm test`.
firstTasks.rationale: The one-file-per-method layout plus SuperTest makes tests approachable, and writing one teaches how the suite drives a real server without risking any production behavior — the safest possible first change.

firstTasks.title: Trace and document one req getter end-to-end
firstTasks.description: Follow a getter such as req.hostname or req.protocol from lib/request.js into the compiled 'trust proxy fn' in lib/utils.js, then improve the inline comment or JSDoc to spell out the trust-proxy interaction you found.
firstTasks.rationale: It forces you to connect the settings system to the request getters — the single most confusing hop for newcomers — while making a small, low-risk, review-friendly documentation improvement.

firstTasks.title: Harden a res.send edge case
firstTasks.description: Study the Content-Length / ETag / freshness logic in res.send (response.js:126-220), find an under-tested branch (e.g. the 205 Reset Content header handling or ArrayBuffer bodies), and add a focused test plus any small fix it reveals.
firstTasks.rationale: res.send is the most central and most-churned source function; working here teaches the HTTP-correctness concerns at the heart of the framework while staying scoped to one method with strong existing test coverage to lean on.

firstTasks.title: Add a new app setting with a compiled companion function
firstTasks.description: Introduce a new setting in the app.set switch (application.js:363) that compiles a helper via a new function in lib/utils.js, following the exact pattern of 'etag'/'query parser'/'trust proxy', and cover it in test/config.js.
firstTasks.rationale: It requires understanding the full settings-compilation mechanism and how defaultConfiguration and app.set interact — a genuine cross-cutting change that touches the framework's core configuration model rather than a leaf method.
